segments.title: A naval historian's design lens for Battlestar Deadlock
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke uses Battlestar Galactica Deadlock's warships to test naval-history lessons against fictional spacecraft design.
segments.body: The game provides a compact way to ask familiar naval questions about armament, protection, aircraft, and fleet roles. Dr. Clarke frames the discussion as a naval historian's response to ships whose design choices can be compared with real-world warship practice.

evidence.note: He introduces the game and says he will consider lessons from naval history and current warship design.

segments.title: Manticore corvettes: useful mass, weak firing arcs
segments.summary: The inexpensive Manticore corvette works as a missile-firing base of fire, but its fore-and-aft guns leave dangerous blind areas.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke likes using many Manticores because they are simple, affordable, missile-equipped all-rounders. His objection is geometric: weapons that cover only the bow and stern invite an opponent to attack from the side, where maneuverability and missile capacity cannot compensate for a missing gun arc.

evidence.note: He praises the Manticore's low cost and missile system, then criticizes its fore-and-aft gun coverage.

segments.title: Real warship gunnery aims for all-round coverage
segments.summary: The Adamant's side-mounted guns prompt a comparison with destroyers designed to keep useful firing arcs across much more of the horizon.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that a spacecraft can use top-and-bottom mounting or carefully placed weapons to cover more approaches than a conventional hull. He contrasts the game's blind zones with Tribal- and Battle-class destroyer arrangements, where mount position was chosen to preserve broad arcs of fire and avoid large undefended sectors.

evidence.note: He discusses Adamant gun placement and compares it with the firing arcs of Tribal- and Battle-class destroyers.

segments.title: The Ranger cruiser as a fragile missile platform
segments.summary: The Ranger carries a useful missile load, but Dr. Clarke finds it too vulnerable to serve comfortably as a close-action cruiser.
segments.body: Rather than exchanging fire directly, he keeps the Ranger at range, weaving it away from enemy guns while it uses missiles. That tactic makes the ship a standoff contributor protected by tougher units, not the robust cruiser he expected when he first unlocked it.

evidence.note: He describes keeping Ranger cruisers outside gun range and relying on their missiles because they feel vulnerable.

segments.title: Why a larger battlestar should carry more aircraft
segments.summary: An Artemis and a larger Jupiter battlestar having the same air group makes little sense to Dr. Clarke's carrier-minded view of force design.
segments.body: He treats aircraft capacity as a central reason to build a larger capital ship, especially in a setting without canal-size restrictions. If the fiction can support larger construction and launch-and-recovery systems, he argues, the newer and larger design should gain meaningful hangar capacity rather than duplicate the older ship's air group.

evidence.note: He questions why Artemis and Jupiter battlestars carry the same air group and argues for expanded hangar space.

segments.title: Standoff missiles meet a fighter-heavy battlespace
segments.summary: Long-range missile combat may suit space, but the game's confined engagements and important fighter threat still demand close-in defences.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke accepts that real space combat might be fought at very long range, much like modern missile warfare at sea. Deadlock compresses the action, however, and makes fighters important enough that he would distribute anti-fighter weapons around a ship's port, starboard, forward, upper, and lower approaches instead of relying on a narrow main armament arc.

evidence.note: He contrasts long-range space combat with the game's close action and calls for broadly distributed anti-fighter weapons.

segments.title: Four Artemis battlestars expose a force-design imbalance
segments.summary: A battle group of Artemis battlestars is powerful because they have all-round fire, but that strength points to a weakness in the rest of the fleet.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke enjoys the formation's combat effectiveness and its ability to engage threats from any direction. His criticism is structural: the first ship in a force with genuinely broad combat coverage should not have to be the principal capital unit, because smaller escorts should also contribute credible all-round fire.

evidence.note: He describes fielding four Artemis battlestars and says broad fire coverage should not begin only with the main capital ship.

segments.title: Flak works, but missiles need a safe path through it
segments.summary: The game makes flak a valuable fighter defence, while raising a practical question about routing friendly missiles through a ship's own barrage.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke finds the flak system satisfying because it resembles the area defence he would expect against attacking aircraft or fighters. He questions the rule that it destroys friendly missiles, suggesting that long-range weapons could be timed or programmed to climb, dive, or otherwise avoid the barrage under a capable fire-control system.

evidence.note: He praises flak's battlefield role and argues that missile flight paths could be deconflicted from it.

segments.title: Boarding threats and combat air patrol
segments.summary: Expected Cylon boarding attacks remain manageable when battlestars keep fighter squadrons close for combat air patrol.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke expected machine opponents willing to accept casualties to make boarding more dangerous than it has proved in his campaign. He explains how independently controlled fighter groups can cover several nearby battlestars, preserving most squadrons for other missions while maintaining a coherent local defence.

evidence.note: He discusses Cylon boarding expectations and describes using two fighter squadrons as close combat air patrol for four battlestars.

segments.title: Viper tactics exploit gaps in ship defence
segments.summary: Vipers are inconsistent in the game, but their flanking and rear attacks underline the cost of leaving gaps in a warship's defensive arcs.
segments.body: When the fighters choose a flank or rear approach, they capitalize on the same coverage failures Dr. Clarke sees in Colonial ship designs. The observation reinforces his larger point that even an advanced force needs layered, all-round defences rather than assuming an enemy will approach its strongest guns.

evidence.note: He describes Vipers' occasional flanking and rear attacks and links their success to gaps in Cylon and Colonial gun coverage.

segments.title: Carrier choices and the Adamant frigate's limitations
segments.summary: The Adamant depends heavily on its fighter squadron, while a battlestar offers the all-round fighting value that makes a separate carrier hard to justify.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke sees little reason to choose a carrier beside a battlestar if the capital ship is the only genuinely versatile combatant available. He returns to the Adamant's limited forward and rear firepower, arguing that a hangar is not an excuse for neglecting the basic defensive arcs of the hull carrying it.

evidence.note: He contrasts carriers with battlestars and revisits the Adamant's dependence on fighters and weak gun coverage.

segments.title: Battlestars should not be the only all-round combatants
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke's final design test is simple: a balanced fleet should not need to field only battlestars to achieve all-round firepower.
segments.body: He identifies the battle star as the game's one consistently capable all-round fighter, while allowing that continued play might revise his assessment. The conclusion remains a naval force-structure critique: a fleet needs useful combat coverage at more than one size and cost level, not a single capital-ship solution to every tactical problem.

evidence.note: He concludes that battlestars should not be the only ships with dependable all-round firepower.
